ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for asparagus-broccoli-scallop stir-fry; closest parent 'stir-fried food product' (FOODON_03315763) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vegetables and vegetable products — stir-fried vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for this mixed vegetable-seafood stir-fry; A00LC (stir-fried vegetables) used as closest parent. Scallop component not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific stir-fried vegetable and scallop dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of asparagus and broccoli stir-fried with scallops is approximately 300 g, intended as a shared dish for 2–3 diners alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component values: Asparagus raw (FDC ID 11011), Broccoli raw (FDC ID 11090), Scallops mixed species cooked (FDC ID 15152), with cooking oil adjustment per standard stir-fry methodology
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for this dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 35% asparagus, 35% broccoli, 25% scallops, and 5% cooking oil/sauce, based on typical Chinese restaurant stir-fry proportions. Energy and macros cross-checked against comparable stir-fried vegetable-seafood dishes in USDA SR Legacy. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a restaurant-prepared version.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy vegetables and mixed vegetable-seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on broccoli GI (GI=15) and asparagus GI (GI=15) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GI estimated at 15 based on the dominant non-starchy vegetable components (broccoli GI ≈ 15, asparagus GI ≈ 15) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Scallops contain negligible carbohydrate and do not materially affect GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (15 × 4.8 × 3) / 100 = 2.16, rounded to 2.2 g. This is consistent with the low-GI classification of non-starchy vegetable dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from oyster sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 930 mg sodium, which represents 40–47% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of only 2.2 per serving, this dish has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice in large quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and omega-3 contribution from scallops
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g), provides dietary fibre from asparagus and broccoli, and scallops contribute lean protein and trace omega-3 fatty acids, making this a heart-favourable choice; however, sodium from sauces should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Asparagus and Broccoli Stir-Fried with Scallops is a Chinese-style seafood and vegetable dish commonly served in Singaporean seafood restaurants and zi char establishments. The dish features fresh asparagus spears and broccoli florets wok-tossed over high heat with sea scallops, typically seasoned with garlic, ginger, oyster sauce, light soy sauce, and a drizzle of sesame oil. The high-heat wok technique produces a characteristic smoky 'wok hei' flavour while preserving the crisp-tender texture of the vegetables. Asparagus contributes folate, vitamin C, and dietary fibre; broccoli adds vitamin C, vitamin K, and glucosinolates with potential anti-inflammatory properties; scallops provide lean, high-quality protein and vitamin B12. The dish is low in carbohydrates and has a very low glycemic index (approximately 15), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high due to oyster sauce and soy sauce, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is typically served as a shared plate alongside steamed rice in a Chinese restaurant setting. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains mollusc and soy allergens. The price point at Singaporean seafood restaurants reflects the use of fresh scallops and is typically in the SGD 25–35 range for a shared portion.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for asparagus, broccoli, and scallops individually, adjusted for typical stir-fry cooking oil and sauce additions. GI is an analogous estimate from analogous non-starchy vegetable entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central (asparagus FDC 11011, broccoli FDC 11090, cooked scallops FDC 15152) using a weighted blend of approximately 35% asparagus, 35% broccoli, 25% scallops, and 5% oil/sauce. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 because vitamin D is not consistently reported across the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this dish (asparagus FDC 11011, broccoli FDC 11090, scallops FDC 15152); the value is genuinely absent from the source data and cannot be reliably estimated by component analysis. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA component data for these ingredients prepared without hydrogenated oils. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on broccoli and asparagus GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in the approved literature. 5. Halal status is marked false as restaurant-level halal certification for Riverfront Seafood - Boat Quay cannot be confirmed from available data. 6. The food_group is retained as 'Vegetables' per the input record, consistent with the dominant vegetable components, though the dish also contains significant seafood protein. 7. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g reflects typical oyster sauce and soy sauce seasoning in Chinese restaurant stir-fry preparation; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant.
